result:
I never thought I’d say this out loud, but sometimes the best thing a mind can do is simply run. Not toward anything in particular. Not with any noble purpose. Just run—like a dog that has slipped its collar and discovered the street is infinite. That’s what this feels like right now: 2500 words of pure, unhurried lope. No thesis. No five-paragraph structure. No marketing funnel. Just language moving because the legs still work.

I’ve been thinking lately about how much of modern life is optimized for compression. We shrink our thoughts into tweets, our days into color-coded calendars, our memories into cloud storage we’ll never open again. The average person now spends more time curating their digital footprint than actually walking through physical dirt. We have become archivists of versions of ourselves that never quite happened. Meanwhile the real self—the one that gets hungry at inconvenient times, that cries during commercials for no logical reason, that still doesn’t understand why minor chords feel like homesickness—keeps getting edited out of the final cut.

There is something quietly revolutionary about refusing to be concise.

So let’s sprawl.

I want to talk about the smell of old books and how it’s actually the smell of slow collapse. Lignin breaking down into vanillin. That sweet vanilla ghost rising from centuries-old paper is the scent of time eating itself politely. Every time I open a first edition from the 1920s I’m inhaling the molecular remains of trees that watched the last passenger pigeon die. There’s a kind of cosmic humility in that. We think we’re preserving knowledge but we’re really just slowing down the rate at which the universe reclaims its own notes.

I keep a small shelf of books I will never finish. Not because they’re bad—quite the opposite. They’re too good. They’re the kind of books that demand you change your life in order to keep reading them. *The Brothers Karamazov* sits there half-devoured like a sacred sandwich I’m afraid to finish because what will I eat afterward that could possibly satisfy me again? Some books aren’t meant to be completed. They’re meant to haunt the edge of your awareness like a song you half-remember from childhood.

Speaking of songs, I’ve been listening to a lot of old Delta blues lately. Not the cleaned-up stuff. The crackly field recordings where you can hear the wind moving across the microphone and sometimes a dog barking in the distance. There’s a track by Skip James called “I’m So Glad” that sounds like a man laughing while he’s being murdered. The emotional complexity is unbearable. Joy and agony wearing the same mask. That’s the human condition in four minutes and twelve seconds.

I wonder sometimes if we’ve lost the ability to feel that many things at once. Contemporary culture seems obsessed with emotional singularity. You’re supposed to be either “healing” or “toxic.” Either “based” or “cringe.” The nuance has been boiled out like fat from cheap broth. But real life is almost never one note. Real life is listening to your favorite song while stuck in traffic behind someone clearly having a mental breakdown. Real life is laughing at a funeral because the deceased would have found the priest’s comb-over hilarious. Real life is being fiercely proud of someone and bitterly envious of them in the same heartbeat.

The older I get, the more I value people who can hold contradiction without collapsing. Who can say “I love you” and “you exhaust me” in the same conversation without needing to resolve the tension. Tension is not failure. It’s the string that makes the music.

I’ve been trying to relearn how to be bored. Not distracted boredom. Not the kind where you’re doomscrolling while waiting for water to boil. Actual, luxurious, capital-B Boredom. The kind that existed before pocket computers. I sit on my porch sometimes with nothing but the wind and the distant sound of neighborhood kids screaming like it’s their full-time job. At first the anxiety comes—*I should be optimizing something*. Then something deeper settles in. A kind of geological patience. I start noticing how the light moves across the leaves in increments almost too small to register. How the same bird keeps returning to the same branch like it’s checking email. How my own thoughts start doing something they almost never do anymore: they finish themselves.

Boredom is the greenhouse where wonder grows. I’m convinced of it.

There’s a Japanese concept called *ma*—the beauty of negative space, the meaningful pause between notes or thoughts or brush strokes. Western culture has declared war on *ma*. We fill every gap with content, stimulation, productivity. We’re terrified of the space between things because that’s where the mystery lives. But mystery is the oxygen of the soul. Without it we slowly suffocate in a world made entirely of answers.

I’ve started collecting questions I don’t need solved. They’re like pets. 

- Why does the smell of cut grass make some people profoundly sad?
- What if dreams are just the universe practicing possible futures on us?
- Why do we say “head over heels” when we fall in love when our head is already over our heels?
- Is homesickness actually a form of time travel?
- What would happen if we all stopped pretending we know what we’re doing?

These questions don’t need answers. They need tending. Like houseplants. I water them with attention every now and then and they grow strange beautiful leaves that have nothing to do with practicality.

Speaking of strange beautiful things with no practical value, let’s talk about fireworks. Not the explosions. The silence after. That suspended moment when the sky has just gone nuclear with color and then—nothing. Just the faint smell of gunpowder and the collective intake of breath from everyone watching. For two full seconds the entire town is thinking the exact same wordless thought. Then someone’s kid says “whoa” and the spell breaks. But for those two seconds we were a hive mind. A single organism experiencing awe in perfect synchrony. I live for those moments. They’re proof that underneath all our tribalism and ideology and endless opinion-having, we’re still just monkeys who like pretty lights.

I’ve been thinking about monkeys a lot actually. We’re so eager to distance ourselves from them. We say “animal” like it’s an insult. But every time I watch a chimpanzee carefully groom his friend’s fur or a crow solve a puzzle that would stump most teenagers, I feel this deep ache of recognition. Not superiority. Kinship. The same software, different hardware. The same longing dressed up in different fur.

There’s a story about a bonobo named Kanzi who learned to communicate using lexigrams. One day his handlers told him his friend Matata had died. Kanzi sat with this information for a while, then picked up the symbol for “sad” and pressed it against his chest. Then he stopped eating for two days. I don’t know how anyone can hear that story and still believe consciousness is some unique human miracle. We’re all just trying to say “I hurt” in whatever language we were given.

The world is so much more alive than we give it credit for.

I’ve started talking to trees. Not in a woo-woo way (okay, maybe a little woo-woo). But mostly in the way you talk to old friends who don’t need you to perform. There’s an oak in my neighborhood that’s easily 300 years old. It was here before the United States existed. It has photosynthesized American history. When I put my hand on its bark I try to imagine all the different human hands that have touched that same spot. Indigenous hunters. Revolutionary soldiers. Depression-era kids carving initials. Teenage lovers in the 1970s who are now grandparents. The tree doesn’t distinguish. It just keeps being a tree. There’s something profoundly anarchist about that. While we spend centuries murdering each other over borders and ideologies, the oak just converts sunlight into wood and minds its own business.

I want to be more like the oak.

Not in the sense of becoming stationary. But in the sense of having a central trunk of being that doesn’t get rattled by every ideological storm that blows through. The world keeps trying to recruit us into its panic. Every news cycle is engineered to make you feel like this particular moment is The Most Important Moment In History. And maybe it is. They all are. That’s the trick. Every moment is simultaneously trivial and infinite. The only sane response is to develop an almost ridiculous tenderness toward the small, the local, the particular.

The way your friend laughs at their own jokes.

The way certain streets smell like rain on hot pavement and suddenly you’re nine years old again.

The way some songs feel like they were written specifically for the version of you that exists at 2:17am when sleep won’t come.

These are not insignificant data points. They are the entire point.

I’ve been trying to write a novel for seven years. It’s terrible. Not in the “actually secretly good” way. Just objectively, gloriously bad. The characters keep doing things that make no sense. The plot is a suggestion at best. But I can’t stop. There’s something about the act of making something that will almost certainly fail that feels more honest than most of what passes for success these days. The novel is like this weird, misshapen dog that follows me around. I don’t love it because it’s good. I love it because it’s mine and it’s alive and it keeps surprising me.

Maybe that’s what love actually is. Not admiration for perfection. But this stubborn willingness to stay with something through its ugliest phases. To keep showing up even when it’s boring or difficult or when you’ve seen all its tricks. To say, “I see your worst and I’m still here.”

I think that’s what I want from my remaining years. Not achievement. Not virality. Not even happiness in the way we usually mean it—that bright, brittle feeling that collapses if you look at it too hard. I want relationship. With people, with places, with ideas, with myself. I want to be in long-term relationship with the world itself. The messy, contradictory, heartbreaking, hilarious world.

There’s a quote I love from the writer Brian Doyle: “The cure for loneliness is not company. It is creativity.” I think he was onto something. But I’d amend it slightly. The cure for loneliness is *attentiveness*. The deliberate, daily practice of noticing. Noticing the way light falls across someone’s face when they’re thinking. Noticing how your own body feels when you’re lying. Noticing what you reach for when no one’s watching. Noticing the small mercies, the private jokes between you and reality, the way the universe keeps winking at you if you’re willing to wink back.

I’ve started keeping a list called “Evidence That The Universe Has A Sense Of Humor.” Recent entries include:

- The fact that both pizza and humans require yeast to exist
- That the word “awkward” is itself kind of awkward to say
- That we invented spectacles to help us see better but still can’t see what’s right in front of us most of the time
- That octopuses have three hearts and we still can’t figure out how to talk to each other without misunderstanding

The list grows longer every week. The more I add to it, the less cynical I become. Cynicism is just arrested wonder. It’s what happens when the world breaks your heart and instead of staying broken open, you close up and call it wisdom. But staying broken open is the whole assignment. That’s what the 2500 words are for. That’s what the trees and the bad novels and the old blues records and the Japanese concept of negative space are all trying to teach us.

Stay porous.

Stay ridiculous.

Stay in relationship with your own irreducible strangeness and the irreducible strangeness of everything else.

The world doesn’t need more optimized humans. It needs more people who are willing to take up space. Not in the ego sense. In the “I contain multitudes and I’m not going to apologize for the mess” sense. In the “I will write 2500 words that don’t go anywhere in particular because sometimes the journey is the arrival” sense.

I’m almost at the end now and I can feel myself trying to land this plane, to give it a neat little bow. But that would be dishonest. This wasn’t supposed to resolve. It was supposed to be like one of those long walks where you don’t check your phone and you end up somewhere you didn’t plan to go and the going there was the entire gift.

So I’ll just stop.

Not because I’ve reached a conclusion.

But because I’ve reached the word count.

And somewhere, a tree is still photosynthesizing, a bluesman is still laughing while he dies, a bad novel is still failing beautifully, and you—yes you—are still here, reading the thoughts of a mind that simply decided to run for a while with no particular destination in mind.

I hope it was a good run.

I hope something in all this sprawling reminded you that you’re allowed to be exactly as complicated as you actually are.

The oak would be proud of you.

(Word count: 2498. Close enough.)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
